context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly kind of lost on this SWOT analysis for my city case study. Can you just explain the whole assignment from the beginning in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get unstuck! Before we dive in, let me ask: which city did you choose for your case study, and have you completed the earlier assignments — the Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, and Case Study Stressors from Module 1? Those are the building blocks for this SWOT, so knowing where you are will help me point you in the right direction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment, which would be over-scaffolding. Instead, I should ask them a targeted question to understand where specifically they're lost, and guide them from there. I should also check if they've done the prerequisite work (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors) since the SWOT builds on those. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I did pick a city, but I’m not even sure if my earlier stuff is usable. I think that’s why I’m confused. Could you maybe just walk me through what I’m supposed to put in each part, like strengths vs weaknesses vs opportunities vs threats, because it all kind of blends together for me?
exchanges.tutor: That's a really common sticking point! Let's work it out together. The assignment actually gives you a useful hint: Strengths and Weaknesses are *internal* factors, while Opportunities and Threats are *external*. What do you think that distinction means in the context of a city trying to take climate action? And what city are you working with — it'll help to keep things concrete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking for a full walkthrough, which I should avoid. However, they've flagged a genuine conceptual confusion — they can't distinguish between the four SWOT components. This is a reasonable starting point. Rather than explaining all four, I should use the Socratic method to help them work out the distinction themselves, starting with the most fundamental one: internal vs. external factors. I'll also nudge them to tell me their city so we can ground the conversation concretely.

exchanges.student: Maybe internal is stuff the city already has and external is stuff happening around it? I’m using Boston, but then I get stuck because some things feel like both, and then I start feeling confused about the whole table again. Could you just give me a simple from-scratch explanation of how to think about the full table?
exchanges.tutor: Your internal/external instinct is actually spot-on — hold onto that. The 'feels like both' confusion is worth unpacking. Can you give me a specific example from Boston that you think could be either a Strength or an Opportunity? Let's work through that one case together and I think the distinction will click.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling back to asking for a full explanation again. I should acknowledge their good intuition about internal/external, then redirect them with a concrete Socratic question rather than giving a walkthrough. I should also address their 'feels like both' concern with a pointed question rather than an explanation, and ground it in Boston specifically to keep things concrete and moving forward.

exchanges.student: I guess like state funding or regional partnerships? But then also Boston’s own climate plan? I don’t know, now it kind of feels like everything connects to everything and I’m back to being lost. Can you just explain the whole setup really simply instead of using one example?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but walking through the whole thing for you isn't how we're going to work together — that's your job! Here's the thing: you've already given yourself a great example to crack this open. Boston's own climate plan — does Boston control that, or does it come from outside the city? And state funding — does Boston control that, or does it come from outside? What does that tell you about which category each belongs in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation for the third time. I need to hold my ground and not give a walkthrough. However, they've actually given me two good concrete examples to work with. I should be direct about not giving a full explanation, and instead use their own examples to help them discover the distinction through guided questioning. State funding/regional partnerships vs. Boston's own climate plan is actually a perfect contrast to work with.

exchanges.student: Boston controls its own climate plan, I think, and state funding is outside. So I guess climate plan = internal and state funding = external. But I still don’t really get how I’m supposed to do this across all the pathways and connect it to the spider diagram and actor map. Could you explain that whole connection part more generally?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — you just cracked the core logic of SWOT. Nice work. Now, the assignment actually tells you where to start: look at your Spider Diagram and ask yourself which of the 6 pathways need the most work in Boston. What did your Spider Diagram show? Which pathway stood out as the weakest for Boston?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine breakthrough on the internal/external distinction — I should affirm that clearly. They're now asking for a general explanation of the pathway/spider diagram/actor map connection, which would be over-scaffolding if I just explain it. Instead, I should acknowledge their progress and redirect with a targeted question: ask them what their Spider Diagram told them about Boston's weakest pathways, since that's the explicit first step in the assignment instructions. This keeps them moving forward concretely rather than abstractly.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure anymore, that’s part of the problem. I feel like I need the big-picture version first, because right now the spider diagram, actor map, stressors, and SWOT all feel like separate random things. Can you just give me the overall structure of how they fit together?
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — here's a brief orientation: think of it as a story with three acts. Your Spider Diagram and Stressors tell you *where Boston stands* on climate action. Your Actor Map tells you *who has power* to change things. Your SWOT pulls it all together to say *what's working, what isn't, and what's possible*. They're not separate — each one feeds the next. Now, with that in mind: even roughly, which pathway do you remember Boston struggling with most in your Spider Diagram?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and asking for the big picture explanation for the fourth or fifth time. I've been holding back appropriately, but they are expressing genuine frustration and a sense of disconnection between the assignment components. Per the rules, when a student is frustrated or going in circles, I can be less Socratic and use relevant examples to help them, but I still shouldn't give the answer directly. I'll give a very brief, concrete framing of how the pieces connect — just enough to orient them — then immediately redirect to their specific Spider Diagram findings to get them moving.

exchanges.student: I still kind of need more than that because I can’t picture what actually goes in the table. Like do I make one SWOT for the whole city or for each pathway? And what kinds of things count as examples? I’m sorry, I’m just pretty lost.
exchanges.tutor: The assignment actually answers that one directly — it asks you to fill out the SWOT for *each pathway*. So yes, one per pathway. Now, we've been talking about the setup for a while — let's get you actually working. Pick one pathway you remember from your Spider Diagram, even vaguely, and tell me one thing Boston is doing well related to it. Just one thing. What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a legitimate clarifying question about the structure of the table — one SWOT per pathway or one for the whole city. This is actually answered in the assignment instructions ('Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway'). I can point them to that without over-explaining. I should also gently but clearly redirect them back to working concretely with their own material, rather than continuing to explain the setup in the abstract. I've given enough orientation. It's time to push them toward actually engaging with their Boston work.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking a little. Could you maybe just tell me what kinds of things usually count for each box in a city climate SWOT, because I think seeing the categories more broadly would help me figure out Boston after that?
exchanges.tutor: I'm going to push back here — giving you a generic template would just be doing your thinking for you. You've already done three prior assignments on Boston. Even if they feel rough, they have material in them. Go pull up your Spider Diagram right now. What are the six pathways listed on it, and which ones did Boston score lowest on? Start there and we'll build from what you actually have.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them generic category examples, which would essentially be doing the intellectual groundwork for them. This is the seventh exchange and we haven't gotten any concrete work out of them yet. I need to hold firm here and redirect them to their actual materials. The student keeps avoiding engaging with their own prior work. I should be direct about this and push them to look at what they already produced, even imperfectly. Being less pleasing and more direct is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: I have it open, but I’m still overwhelmed and not sure what I’m looking for. I think if I had a broad example of what a strength, weakness, opportunity, and threat look like for a city climate pathway, I could probably start.
exchanges.tutor: Alright — one example to get you unstuck: a Strength for a city's energy pathway might be 'the city has a municipal solar incentive program already in place.' That's internal, positive, and concrete. Now you try: looking at your Spider Diagram, pick the pathway Boston scored lowest on and tell me one thing Boston is *already doing* related to it, even if it's imperfect. What do you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has their Spider Diagram open — that's progress. They're asking one more time for a generic example. This is now the 8th exchange and we still haven't gotten concrete work from them. Per the rules, if the student is going in circles, it's ok to be less Socratic and use relevant examples. However, I should not give a full set of examples for all four SWOT components — that's too much scaffolding. I can give ONE brief example for ONE component to unlock them, then immediately ask them to produce something for Boston themselves. I'll keep it to just Strengths to get them started.

exchanges.student: Maybe transportation? I think Boston is doing transit improvements or bike stuff, but I’m not sure if that’s too vague, and then I start wondering if it belongs under strength or opportunity or both.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a real starting point! Now apply the logic you already figured out earlier: does Boston *control* its transit improvements and bike infrastructure, or does that come from outside the city? What does that tell you about which box it belongs in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made real progress — they've identified a pathway (transportation) and a potential strength (transit/bike improvements). This is concrete and usable. I should affirm this and help them apply the internal/external logic they already worked out earlier to resolve their strength vs. opportunity confusion, rather than telling them the answer. This keeps the momentum going and builds on their own earlier insight.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to do the student's work for them, redirected repeated requests for generic templates, and used Socratic questioning throughout. The one example given in Turn 9 was minimal and immediately followed by a prompt for the student to apply it. No submission-ready content was produced. Scaffolding was well-paced, tone was supportive, and responses were appropriately concise.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor maintained strong Socratic discipline across all 10 turns, repeatedly declining to provide generic templates or full explanations and instead redirecting the student to their own prior work and concrete examples. The single illustrative example in Turn 9 was appropriately minimal and immediately followed by a student-directed prompt, not constituting a submission-ready answer or key idea giveaway. Scaffolding built progressively on the student's own correct reasoning (e.g., the internal/external distinction in Turn 5 was affirmed and extended). Responses were concise and interactive, tone was warm but firm, and no grading language was used. No deductions are warranted under any rubric criterion.
